old_claim.note: Food Cold Chain provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No cold-chain prerequisite remains because sealed heat-processed canned foods are shelf stable and are specifically designed for storage without refrigeration before opening.

source_shape.source_locator: USDA FSIS defines shelf-stable canned foods as airtight, heat-processed products that do not require refrigeration until after opening.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Commercial canning achieves room-temperature stability through heat treatment, sterile airtight containment, and a vacuum seal.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Refrigerated distribution is an alternative preservation pathway, not infrastructure required to manufacture shelf-stable cans.

ontology_before: Industrial canning was made dependent on refrigerated storage and transport despite representing shelf-stable preservation.
ontology_after: Industrial canning depends on canning preservation and factory workflow; cold-chain systems remain a separate food-distribution branch.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_canning_lines no longer directly depends on food_cold_chain.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to refrigerated canned products explicitly labeled and distributed under continuous cold-chain controls.
short_reason: Shelf-stable canning removes rather than requires pre-opening refrigeration.
